stories.headline: Measles case confirmed in DIA passenger who attended Littleton church service
stories.summary:
A person with a confirmed measles case traveled through Denver International Airport on Saturday and attended a service at St. Mary's Catholic Church in Littleton while infectious, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment announced Tuesday. The passenger arrived at DIA at 9:15 a.m. on Frontier flight 1279 from Miami, then attended the church service and reception at 6853 S. Prince St. from 9:30 a.m. to 2 p.m. Sunday before departing DIA again late Sunday night on a return flight to Miami.

Anyone at DIA between 9:15 a.m. and noon Saturday, or between 9:30 p.m. Sunday and 1:30 a.m. Monday, may have been exposed. Because the passenger was fully vaccinated, public health officials said the risk of transmission is lower but still possible, and passengers on both flights will be notified by their state or local public health agency.

Free measles vaccination clinics were scheduled Wednesday at the Arapahoe County Public Health Department, 4857 S. Broadway in Englewood, and at St. Mary's Catholic Church. Officials noted that receiving a vaccine within 72 hours of exposure may prevent measles or reduce symptom severity.

Colorado recorded 36 measles cases in 2025, the highest single-year total since at least 1996, with most cases among unvaccinated individuals. The current case is the first confirmed in Colorado in 2026 and is located in Arapahoe County.

stories.headline: Grass fire in Thornton injures five, forces evacuations and I-25 closures
stories.summary:
A vegetation fire ignited at approximately 11:30 a.m. Wednesday at 84th Avenue and Huron Street in Thornton amid Red Flag conditions, spreading rapidly eastward through a green belt between a residential neighborhood and a commercial zone before being fully contained at about 2:07 p.m. at roughly 10 acres. Thornton Fire Chief Stephen Kelley described the blaze as a dynamic, rapidly spreading event that required a large-scale response drawing resources from Thornton, Westminster, Adams County, North Denver Metro, Northglenn, Federal Heights, South Adams County, and other jurisdictions.

Law enforcement conducted door-to-door evacuations of nearby residents, and a high school in the area was fully evacuated as a precaution. Multiple roads were closed due to fire and smoke conditions, including a stretch of Interstate 25 where visibility was severely limited by billowing smoke.

Five people were injured in connection with the fire — four firefighters and one civilian — all with non-critical injuries, according to Chief Kelley. No residential structures were lost, though some businesses along the southern perimeter of the fire sustained damage, which was not believed to be structural.

Crews remained on scene overnight to monitor hotspots and prevent reignition. The cause of the fire remains under investigation.

stories.headline: Man arrested in Kansas on suspicion of murder in Denver Valentine's Day shooting
stories.summary:
A 20-year-old man suspected of fatally shooting another man at a Denver party on Valentine's Day was arrested Friday in Kansas, Denver police said. Yeanbraiker Yriarte-Valera was booked into the Wyandotte County Detention Center on a Denver homicide warrant and faces charges of first-degree murder, four counts of attempted first-degree murder, four counts of first-degree assault, and a violent crime sentence enhancer, according to Denver court records.

Denver police responded to the shooting in the 1500 block of West Maple Avenue at approximately 5:15 a.m. on Feb. 14, finding a woman shot in the ankle and a man who died at the scene. Investigators believe a fight at a party escalated into the shooting, with at least two people firing shots.

Yriarte-Valera is believed to have fired the bullet that killed the male victim. A second suspect, believed to have shot and injured the female victim, had not been publicly identified as of Tuesday.

Yriarte-Valera's first Colorado court date had not been scheduled as of Tuesday. Anyone with information is asked to contact Metro Denver Crime Stoppers at 720-913-7867.

stories.headline: Two defendants plead guilty to second-degree murder in 2024 Denver Aquarium shooting
stories.summary:
Two men accused of killing 19-year-old Dacien Salazar in a drive-by shooting outside the Denver Aquarium on Valentine's Day 2024 have each pleaded guilty to second-degree murder, the Denver District Attorney's Office confirmed. Jason Trujillo, 19, pleaded guilty Jan. 16, and Antonio Vasquez, 21, entered his plea Dec. 19, 2025. Both are scheduled to be sentenced March 6.

Salazar and two friends had traveled from Pueblo to the Denver Aquarium when a person wearing a black mask in a passing black sedan opened fire. Salazar was transported to a hospital, where he later died. Investigators linked the shooting to prior Facebook threats against Salazar, including one involving Trujillo, and connected it to a separate non-injury shooting in Pueblo.

Trujillo and Vasquez were arrested in May 2024 on first-degree murder charges. Cell phone records, surveillance footage from a nearby hotel, and an anonymous tip helped investigators identify the pair.

The case drew significant public attention after a reward for information was increased in mid-April 2024 during the months-long search for suspects.

stories.headline: Denver Pavilions' future to be shaped by Urban Land Institute advisory panel this spring
stories.summary:
The Denver Downtown Development Authority has engaged the Urban Land Institute to convene an advisory services panel that will visit Denver from April 13–17 and produce recommendations on the future of the Denver Pavilions mall and adjacent parking lots along 16th and 15th streets. The DDDA is paying ULI $100,000 for the panel's work, and a report is expected approximately one month after the visit, according to Denver Chief Projects Officer Bill Mosher.

The DDDA purchased the 350,000-square-foot mall in December for $37 million and the two adjacent parking lots for $23 million, after the previous owner, Gart Properties, defaulted on the property's loan. The mall is approximately 60 percent leased and generates $5.3 million annually in rent, while the parking operations bring in an additional $3.2 million per year.

Ahead of the ULI visit, the DDDA has issued two requests for proposals — one seeking retail and entertainment leasing specialists to develop a repositioning vision, and another soliciting architecture and urban planning firms to reimagine the property and adjacent lots as a centerpiece of a revitalized Upper Downtown. Both RFP submissions are due March 13.

Mosher said the DDDA's preferred outcome is to transfer the property to the private sector as quickly as possible, and the authority does not intend to execute major new leases in the interim. Gart Properties continues to manage the mall for $379,000 under the current arrangement.

stories.headline: Historic Loretto Heights administration building to become 50 rent-to-own affordable apartments
stories.summary:
Developer Commún plans to convert Denver's historic Administration Building at Loretto Heights into 50 rent-to-own apartments as part of a broader $70 million campus redevelopment effort. The first two floors of the building are slated for community programming, including a business incubator, while remaining floors will house affordable housing units.

A historic chapel attached to the building will be preserved and continue operating as a wedding and event venue. The project represents a rare application of a rent-to-own model in Denver's affordable housing landscape, offering tenants a pathway to homeownership.

The Loretto Heights campus, a former Catholic college in southwest Denver, has been the subject of ongoing redevelopment discussions for several years. The administration building conversion is one of the most prominent components of the broader effort to reactivate the site.

The project comes as Denver's construction costs rank second-highest in the nation, according to a 2026 report from the Beck Group, driven largely by luxury projects and complex downtown developments.

stories.headline: $132 million affordable housing development breaks ground in Denver's Globeville neighborhood
stories.summary:
Evergreen Real Estate Group and Globeville Redevelopment Partners have broken ground on a $132 million mixed-use affordable housing development in Denver's Globeville neighborhood. The project will transform a city-owned parcel on the site of a former car dealership into housing for families earning between 30 and 80 percent of the area median income.

Globeville is one of Denver's most historically underserved and environmentally burdened neighborhoods, situated near the confluence of I-25 and I-70 and adjacent to industrial land uses. The redevelopment of a former commercial site into income-restricted housing represents a significant investment in a community that has faced persistent disinvestment.

Details on the number of units, timeline to completion, and specific community amenities were not fully disclosed in available materials. The project is among the larger affordable housing investments announced in the Denver metro in recent months.

stories.headline: Colorado business leaders react to Supreme Court tariff ruling, warn further action likely
stories.summary:
Within hours of a 6-3 U.S. Supreme Court ruling striking down tariffs imposed under emergency powers law, Denver and Colorado business leaders said the decision provided needed clarity while cautioning that the administration is likely to pursue alternative trade measures. Denver Metro Chamber of Commerce President J.J. Ament said the ruling establishes defined lanes for policymakers while underscoring the importance of predictability and stability for businesses.

The tariffs had significantly affected multiple Colorado industries, including wheat producers, bicycle retailers, and automotive dealerships. The head of the Colorado Automobile Dealers Association said it supports measures that make vehicles more affordable for consumers.

Colorado Chamber of Commerce President Loren Furman said members across sectors had experienced supply chain disruptions, increased overhead costs, and uncertainty, and expressed hope the ruling would offer some relief. However, Matthew Groves of a Colorado business group warned that the administration retains multiple other avenues to apply tariffs and characterized the ruling as temporary relief.

President Trump announced plans to sign an executive order imposing a 10 percent global tariff rate on top of existing tariffs hours after the court's decision, saying he disagreed with the ruling but had powerful alternatives available.

stories.headline: Laura Clellan confirmed as first female Colorado Parks and Wildlife director since 2011 merger
stories.summary:
Colorado Parks and Wildlife commissioners unanimously confirmed Laura Clellan as the agency's permanent director Monday, making her the first woman to hold the role since CPW was formed by a merger of two predecessor agencies in 2011. Clellan had been serving as acting director since Dec. 1 and was selected from a pool of roughly 150 applicants.

Clellan brings more than three decades of military service, including overseas deployments in Afghanistan, and previously served as adjutant general and executive director of the Colorado Department of Military and Veterans Affairs. She achieved the rank of major general in the Colorado Army National Guard and earlier worked with the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service in Lakewood.

She assumes the role at a contentious moment for the agency, with the federal government having halted Colorado's wolf reintroduction program and ongoing disputes over furbearer management. Some hunting groups have expressed concern that the Polis administration is sidelining their interests through appointments and policy decisions, though the executive director of Coloradans for Responsible Wildlife Management said his group is confident Clellan will lead with transparency and accountability.

Conservation groups expressed cautious optimism. Rob Edward of the Rocky Mountain Wolf Project said his organization has high expectations of the new director, while the Center for Biological Diversity said it hopes Clellan's leadership will broaden how the agency approaches wildlife management beyond a purely economic lens.

stories.headline: Broncos bring back John Morton as offensive passing-game coordinator ahead of 2026 season
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos have hired John Morton as offensive passing-game coordinator, completing a significant retooling of the team's offensive staff heading into head coach Sean Payton's fourth season, a source confirmed to The Denver Post. Morton previously served in the same role for Denver in 2023 and 2024 before leaving for an offensive coordinator position with the Detroit Lions in 2025.

Morton's return is partly a reunion: Payton brought him back to Denver as a consultant during the Broncos' recent playoff run after the Lions stripped Morton of play-calling duties midseason and ultimately fired him following a 9-8 season. Morton contributed immediately upon his return, helping design a goal-line play for offensive tackle Frank Crum that resulted in a touchdown during Denver's 33-30 overtime divisional win over the Buffalo Bills.

The hire caps a series of offensive staff changes Payton initiated after firing offensive coordinator Joe Lombardi two days following the Broncos' AFC Championship Game loss. Payton promoted quarterbacks coach Davis Webb to offensive coordinator, elevated Logan Kilgore to the quarterbacks coaching role, and hired Ronald Curry as wide-receivers coach before finalizing Morton's addition.

Morton has a long history with Payton dating to 2006, when he served as receivers coach during Payton's first season with the New Orleans Saints. Whether Morton will have a play-calling role alongside Webb in 2026 remains to be determined.

stories.headline: Heat pump installations in Colorado more than doubled in 2025, reaching market tipping point
stories.summary:
The number of heat pumps installed across the service areas of five major Colorado utilities surpassed 14,225 in 2025, more than doubling year-over-year and representing a twelvefold increase over three years, according to new data from the Energy Efficiency Business Coalition. Industry executives and installers say the technology has reached a market tipping point, driven by improved contractor familiarity, favorable rebate stacking, and competitive pricing against traditional furnaces and air conditioners.

Xcel Energy, Colorado's largest gas and electricity supplier, issued 10,640 heat pump rebates worth $57 million in 2025, with Denver, Boulder, and Littleton among the most active areas. The Platte River Power Authority, which serves Fort Collins, Longmont, Loveland, and Estes Park, saw its heat pump rebates nearly quadruple to 1,256, totaling $2.2 million.

Air-source heat pumps use a refrigerant with an extremely low boiling point to extract heat from outdoor air even in subzero temperatures, then distribute it indoors. In summer, the system runs in reverse to function as an air conditioner. Installed costs range from $15,000 to $30,000 depending on system size.

Nationally, 3.642 million heat pumps were sold in the U.S. in 2025, surpassing sales of natural gas and oil furnaces for the second consecutive year. The coalition's goal is for heat pumps to capture 50 to 80 percent of the total Colorado heating and cooling market by 2030.

stories.headline: MSU Denver launches fellowship to support and retain Black male educators in metro-area schools
stories.summary:
Metropolitan State University of Denver has launched the Black Male Educator Fellowship, known as "The Den," aimed at supporting Black male teachers within Denver Public Schools and Aurora Public Schools through professional development, networking, and community building. The program is a collaboration between MSU Denver's Call Me MiSTER initiative, the university's Office of Education Solutions, both school districts, and the Colorado Men of Color Collaborative.

Dr. Rashad Anderson, director of Call Me MiSTER at MSU Denver, said isolation is a significant factor driving Black male educators out of the classroom, noting that Black men make up approximately 6 percent of Colorado's population and that many Black teachers are the only educator of color in their building. Anderson said he has observed pressure placed on Black teachers to serve as disciplinary problem-solvers for students of color, a burden that compounds an already demanding profession.

The fellowship addresses a stark statewide shortage: according to the National Association of State Boards of Education, 40 percent of Colorado school districts do not employ a single teacher of color, and 62 percent have no Black male teachers at all. Program participant Kaheem Heath, an aspiring teacher, said he has never had a Black male teacher in his own schooling and views recruitment efforts as essential to changing that reality for future students.

Research consistently shows that students of all backgrounds benefit from exposure to educators with diverse identities and experiences. Fellowship organizers said the program is intended not only to retain current educators but to inspire the next generation of Black male teachers in Colorado.
